surfaces.summary: The runtime v1 entity validators are tightly pared against their JSON Schemas with only a handful of divergences. The hand-authored validators for the 13 entities are in excellent shape; the authoring/v1 and authoring/v2 codegen-generated layer is also clean. Four concrete parity defects were found: a strict/open-world mismatch in EvalSpec.scoring that will silently reject valid tool-extension payloads; missing minLength:1 enforcement on four string fields (license, author, compatibility, description) in both v1 and v2 skill-frontmatter Zod validators; the NORMATIVE Blueprint B § 7.4 gate_reasons non-empty invariant is documented in the JSON Schema description but enforced in neither the JSON Schema allOf nor the Zod superRefine; and EvidenceStatement/EvidenceBundlePayload ship as exported Zod validators with no corresponding JSON Schema file on disk and no registration in schemas/v1/index.json.

The scripts and gate-chain are well-structured with no exit-code swallowing, proper use of `&&`-chaining, and fail-closed semantics throughout. The codegen is deterministic: it re-derives output from frozen JSON schemas, uses `guardedWrite` to protect the byte-frozen v1 schema tree, and the `--check` idempotency gate is correctly wired into both `pnpm run check` and CI. Two real defects were found: the predicate-namespace isolation gate defaults to scanning only `schemas/authoring/v1`, leaving the new `schemas/authoring/v2` family uncovered in both the local gate chain and CI; and the v1 byte-freeze test omits five of the seven v1 validator source files from its frozen-path list, so `codegen:authoring` in write mode can silently overwrite those files if codegen logic ever changes.

The core/checks/evidence packages are well-structured with solid Zod schema enforcement, deterministic exit codes, and careful invariant checking on evidence statements. Two correctness issues stand out: the judgment engine silently drops the `params` argument when dispatching built-in deterministic checks, making `contains`, `regex_match`, `min_length`, and `max_length` pass vacuously on any criterion that uses them from the engine path; and the CISO gate timeout implementation sets a flag but never aborts the underlying `invokeProvider()` awaitable, so a hung adapter will block the caller indefinitely rather than return after `timeoutMs`. The evidence writer has a minor but silent data-shape inconsistency in the `array` format path.

The emit + signing scope is generally careful: the CLI refuses to sign without --artifact, verifies the artifact's sha256 against predicate.input_hash before invoking cosign, validates every row against a strict() Zod schema (blocking field injection via untrusted metadata), cleans up temp dirs in finally, and the dashboard emit script fails closed on kernel-invalid bundles. Two falsifiable defects exist: the default --sign path double-wraps the attestation by feeding cosign the full Statement as its predicate body (the in-code comment documents the inverse of what the code does), and the coverage-floor detection regex over-matches any `lines:` token in vitest.config.ts, which can flip honest advisory evidence into a fabricated signed pass/fail.

The watcher infrastructure is architecturally sound — the three-layer detection model (byte-hash, field-diff, liveness streaks) is well-thought-out and the injection whitelist guards in validate-trilink.sh and bd-claim-precheck.sh are correctly implemented. The two weakest spots are: (1) a class of extractors that silently hash empty input when their grep pattern matches nothing, producing a stable sha256(empty) baseline that never detects drift after an upstream structural change, and (2) the RATIFIED-WITH-DELTAS gate in bd-claim-precheck.sh that documents its own bypass path in the denial message, defeating its stated enforcement purpose. A retired ntfy topic name means spec-drift phone alerts are silently dropped.

The Python scoring scripts are well-structured and follow the advisory-first, fail-open design consistently across all six files. The CRAP formula itself (C^2 * (1-cov)^3 + C) is implemented correctly, fp-rate.py has proper division-by-zero guards, and currency.py's parse/report logic is clean. However, three of the four language backends in crap-score.py produce systematically wrong CRAP scores in production: the Go test-kind backend issues a gocyclo regexp that matches all .go files (yielding permanently empty test scores), the Go src-kind backend has a path-format mismatch between gocyclo relative paths and go-tool-cover module-qualified paths (so all Go methods score at 0% coverage), and the JS backend has the same absolute-vs-relative path mismatch against coverage-summary.json. Rust hardcodes coverage=0.0 despite the comment promising tarpaulin integration. These bugs produce silently inflated CRAP scores (or silently suppressed test scores for Go) that affect gate pass/fail decisions on real repos — the golden test suite does not catch them because it was intentionally designed for the no-provider code path.

Exit-code propagation through the Node dispatcher (bin/audit-harness.js) is mechanically correct: child exit codes pass through verbatim via process.exit(code ?? 0), signal handling is separated, and timeout exits as 124. The underlying shell scripts are well-structured with set -euo pipefail throughout and || true guards on intentionally-ignorable grep calls. Three concrete defects were found: the CI escape-scan self-check is permanently dark due to a pipeline/tee exit-code masking bug, arch-check.sh silently degrades provenance hashes on macOS by using sha256sum directly without the cross-platform SHA256_CMD pattern every other script implements, and emit-evidence.sh produces structurally broken JSON in its OTEL diagnostic output when AUDIT_HARNESS_SIDE contains a double-quote.

The validator core is structurally sound: tier routing is correct (TIER_ENTERPRISE and TIER_MARKETPLACE are the same constant so all tier comparisons are consistent), the ALWAYS_REQUIRED 8-field set correctly generates errors at marketplace tier per SCHEMA_CHANGELOG NON-NEGOTIABLES, and the kernel-shadow script is clean advisory-only infrastructure. Three real defects were found: (1) validate_tool_permission always returns True so malformed allowed-tools entries silently pass at all tiers; (2) missing name at standard tier produces zero diagnostic output despite the field being listed in STANDARD_REQUIRED; (3) a stale docstring cluster at lines 13-17, 126-127, and 140 incorrectly describes marketplace-tier enforcement as warnings-only when the code has correctly errored on all 8 ALWAYS_REQUIRED fields since schema 3.3.0. The sync-marketplace.cjs DISALLOWED_KEYS blocklist does not strip any ALWAYS_REQUIRED fields and is clean. No kernel-shadow.mjs file exists at the repo root; the shadow logic lives in scripts/kernel-shadow-validation.mjs and is well-engineered.

The 31 workflows are, on the whole, security-conscious for a high-traffic public repo: the one pull_request_target workflow (pr-prescreen.yml) is correctly hardened (PR head checked out with persist-credentials:false into ./pr, all executable scripts and the validator run FROM the trusted base/main checkout, never from PR content), publish/release/deploy workflows are gated to push-to-main or tag/dispatch with constrained inputs, and untrusted PR fields (title/body) are consistently passed via env: rather than interpolated into run:. The one real defect is a GitHub Actions template-injection in sync-external.yml where repository_dispatch client_payload.source is interpolated directly into a run: block. Secondary supply-chain softness (unpinned trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main, mutable mcp-publisher "latest" download) exists only in schedule/tag/dispatch-triggered jobs with read scope, so it is not a fork-PR exposure.

The action.yml is a deliberately minimal bootstrap stub (v0.0.0) that emits decision=not-implemented and exits 0. The injection-hardening posture is sound: inputs pass through env vars and are consumed by jq with --arg, not shell string concatenation. Two real defects exist: (1) `actions/checkout@v6` and `actions/setup-node@v6` are referenced in ci.yml but v6 does not exist for either action — the latest stable versions are v4 and v4 respectively, meaning CI runs against a non-existent tag and silently falls back or errors; (2) three inputs (predicate-uri, rekor-url, cosign-key) are declared in action.yml but never passed into the env block, so any downstream behavior that attempts to consume them will silently receive empty/default values. The bead docket is uniformly consistent with the repo state: M5 is not started, @j-rig/rollout-gate@2.0.0 is not published, and virtually all 59 beads are legitimately blocked on either M5 implementation work or the upstream j-rig v2.0.0 release.

confirmed_findings.claim: The judgment engine calls runCheck without forwarding any params argument, causing all built-in parameterized checks to evaluate against their silent defaults and pass vacuously.
confirmed_findings.trigger: Any EvalSpec criterion with method='deterministic' and deterministic_check='contains' (or 'regex_match', 'min_length', 'max_length') is dispatched via judgeDeterministic; no params are passed. The 'contains' check resolves needle to '' and every string includes '', so every input passes. 'regex_match' resolves pattern to '' and every string matches. 'min_length' resolves min to 0 and every string length >= 0 passes.
confirmed_findings.fix_sketch: Either (a) add a params field to CriterionSchema and thread it through judgeDeterministic -> runCheck, or (b) remove the vacuous defaults so missing params returns an error CheckResult (severity='error') rather than silently passing. Option (b) is safer as a fast fix: in each built-in check, throw or return false when the required param is absent.
confirmed_findings.verify_reasoning: Verified on origin/main worktree. /tmp/review-worktrees/jrig-main/packages/core/src/judgment/engine.ts line 50 reads exactly: `const checkResult = runCheck(criterion.deterministic_check, outcome.output.text);` — no third (params) argument, while runCheck's signature in checks/deterministic-registry.ts:28-32 is `runCheck(checkName: string, input: string, params?: Record<string, unknown>)`. confirmed_findings.claim: The G-1 (and G-2) timeout implementation sets a flag after timeoutMs but does not cancel the awaited invokeProvider(); a hung adapter causes runCisoGateG1 to block indefinitely.
confirmed_findings.trigger: A provider adapter that never resolves its completion promise (network hang, infinite retry loop, blocked I/O) is passed as invokeProvider. setTimeout fires at timeoutMs and sets timedOut=true, but 'await args.invokeProvider()' is still outstanding. The function never proceeds past the await, the finally block never runs, and process.stdout.write remains patched permanently in the calling process.
confirmed_findings.fix_sketch: Use Promise.race with an explicit rejection after timeoutMs: wrap invokeProvider() in a race against a timeout-rejection promise. The timeout branch should also restore the originals inside the race loser's cleanup via a finally on the wrapping race. The same fix applies identically to g2-env-var-spillover.ts.
confirmed_findings.verify_reasoning: Verified on origin/main. g1-credential-redaction.ts:107-122 reads exactly as quoted: `let timedOut = false; const timeoutHandle = setTimeout(() => { timedOut = true; }, timeoutMs);` then `try { await args.invokeProvider(); } catch ... finally { clearTimeout(timeoutHandle); process.stdout.write = origStdoutWrite; process.stderr.write = origStderrWrite; }`. confirmed_findings.trigger: Run crap-score.py on any Go repo with --target test (or --target both). confirmed_findings.claim: For Go repos, complexity paths from gocyclo (relative filesystem paths like 'pkg/file.go') never match coverage paths from 'go tool cover -func' (module-qualified paths like 'github.com/user/repo/pkg/file.go'), so every Go method silently uses coverage=0.0 and CRAP is computed as if coverage is zero.
confirmed_findings.trigger: Run crap-score.py on any Go module that is not a flat single-package repo. gocyclo with cwd=root and target='.' outputs relative paths ('pkg/file.go'). go tool cover -func reads coverage.out and outputs module-qualified paths ('github.com/user/repo/pkg/file.go'). The dict lookup `coverage.get(fpath, 0.0)` on line 203 always misses, so all Go methods receive coverage=0.0 and their CRAP scores are the maximum possible (C^2 + C), artificially inflating FAIL counts.

repo_health.summary: Validators and gate-chain are tight, codegen is deterministic, and the SAK shipped through v0.5.0 on schedule with strong bead hygiene (multiple verified close-nows). Four parity defects (strict/open scoring mismatch, unenforced NORMATIVE gate_reasons invariant, missing minLength on 4 skill-frontmatter fields, unregistered Evidence schemas) plus two gate-coverage gaps (authoring/v2 uncovered by the namespace gate, byte-freeze omits 5 of 7 validator files) keep it off an A.

A confirmed P0 in the judgment engine — params silently dropped, so contains/regex_match/min_length/max_length pass vacuously — undermines the product's core correctness claim, compounded by a hangable CISO-gate timeout, a double-wrapping default --sign path, and an over-matching coverage-floor regex that can fabricate signed evidence. The shipped surface is otherwise solid (MM-1..6, CISO gates G-1/G-2, AGENTS.md parser, stub discipline) and 15 beads verified done, but nothing should publish as v2.0.0 until the P0/P1s land.

Watcher architecture is sound and the spec-drift machinery (16 surfaces, daily cron, field-diff, liveness) is the most advanced continuity infrastructure in the ecosystem, but a class of extractors silently hashes empty grep output (permanent drift blindness after upstream structural change), the claim-precheck gate prints its own bypass, and a retired ntfy topic silently drops drift alerts. Bead docket carries heavy rescope debt: four planned doc-number slots were stolen by later files and several quarterly asks are superseded by daily crons.

Three of four CRAP language backends produce systematically wrong scores in production (Go test scores permanently empty, Go and JS coverage always read 0%, Rust hardcoded to 0.0) and the golden suite was deliberately built around the no-provider path so it cannot catch any of it; the CI escape-scan self-check is also permanently dark from a tee exit-code mask. The dispatcher, bash layer, and npm release pipeline are well-built — this is a correctness failure in the scoring core of a measurement tool, not a structural one.

The v0.0.0 stub is honest, injection-hardened (env + jq --arg), and its 59-bead docket is uniformly consistent with reality — everything legitimately blocked on M5 work or the unpublished @j-rig/rollout-gate@2.0.0. Two real but small defects: ci.yml references non-existent actions/checkout@v6 and setup-node@v6, and three declared inputs (predicate-uri, rekor-url, cosign-key) are never wired into the env block.

The validator core honors the SCHEMA_CHANGELOG non-negotiables (8-field ALWAYS_REQUIRED errors at marketplace tier, consistent tier routing) and 30 of 31 workflows are properly hardened including the pull_request_target path. But validate_tool_permission always returns True so malformed allowed-tools pass silently at every tier, missing name at standard tier emits zero diagnostics, and sync-external.yml carries a live template injection from repository_dispatch client_payload.source.

synthesis.narrative:
Six repos, 443 beads, 10 surfaces, everything adversarially verified. The headline: the platform's two measurement engines are both lying in production. j-rig's judgment engine drops the params argument, so every built-in deterministic check — contains, regex_match, min_length, max_length — passes vacuously. That's a P0 in the exact code that is supposed to be the trust anchor. audit-harness's CRAP scorer is wrong on three of four language backends (Go test scores permanently empty, Go and JS coverage always read 0%), and the golden suite was deliberately designed around the no-provider path, so CI stays green while the numbers are garbage. Both repos look healthy from the outside because every failure mode is silent.

Second tier: the evidence/signing chain has integrity bugs that corrupt the product story without being catastrophic — j-rig's default --sign double-wraps the attestation, the coverage-floor regex can flip honest advisory evidence into a fabricated signed pass/fail, the kernel doesn't enforce its own NORMATIVE gate_reasons invariant, and the lab's drift-watch extractors can go permanently blind by hashing empty grep output. Fix all of this before publishing anything new — especially before @j-rig/rollout-gate@2.0.0, which would ship the engine P0 to the one consumer waiting on it.

Bead hygiene is better than feared: 331 keep, 39 verifiably done and awaiting serialized closes, 6 duplicates with clear survivors, 62 rescopes — mostly blown version anchors (v0.24.0, v0.3.x, v0.4.0 targets that no longer exist) and doc-number slots stolen by later files. Zero unverified P0/P1 claims survived review. intent-rollout-gate has the cleanest docket in the portfolio: 59 beads, all legitimately blocked on j-rig 2.0.0, no rot.

Sequence: j-rig P0 first, then the P1 cluster per repo, then merge j-rig #103 and re-judge its five beads, then publish @j-rig/rollout-gate@2.0.0 — one release unblocks the largest blocked docket. bd closes run serialized with JSONL flushes.

Five human decisions pending, none blocking the P0 work: flip required status checks on three repos (long past the 3-green-day trigger), PyPI/crates scope for audit-harness, whether the GH-issue projection satisfies the Plane-on-drift ask under DR-028 T3, @j-rig publish scope (rollout-gate only vs workspace), and whether the four displaced lab standards docs get authored at 051+ or formally deferred.
